coredata.dc:title: Self-sacrificing carbon electrode-controlled oxidation-enhanced water electrolysis for co-production of low-cost hydrogen and carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Ruzhan
Li, Na
Ji, Nana
Li, Dexu
Liu, Quansheng
Zhou, Huacong
Teng, Yingyue
Hao, Jianxiu
Ban, Yanpeng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-assisted water electrolysis can reduce the energy consumption of hydrogen production. In traditional carbon-assisted water electrolysis, carbon particles are directly introduced into the anode cell, but the speed at which these carbon particles are oxidized limits the hydrogen production efficiency of this process. Moreover, the excessive oxidation of carbon particles can produce CO2, leading to greater carbon emissions. Herein, a novel sacrificial anode (SA) based on the carbon particles in coal liquefaction residue is prepared. The carbon-oxygen reaction is controlled on the surface of SA to increase the hydrogen generation rate, and carbon quantum dots (CDs) are also produced by this reaction. A DFT investigation reveals that carbon atoms with a high spin density lower the adsorption energy of the reactive oxygen intermediate ·OH during the transition from OH− to ·OH. Carbon-rich clusters on the sacrificial anode are in-situ oxidized by ·OH to form CDs. Additionally, at a potential of 1.80 V, the energy consumption required for hydrogen production can be reduced to 2.15–2.58 kWh/Nm3, and the corresponding yield of CDs is as high as 13.66 wt%. This research provides a new horizon for large-scale, low-cost, and green hydrogen production via carbon-assisted water electrolysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-assisted water electrolysis
Carbon sacrificial anode
Carbon oxidation reaction
Hydrogen production
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical and catalytic activity of SA
Evolution of electrode structure and clarification of COR mechanisms of sacrificial anode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the self-supporting carbon electrode preparation process.

Material characterization of SA. (a) SEM image of C*-12; (b–d) TEM images of C*-12; (e) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of CLR and C*-T; (f) pore size distributions of CLR and C*-T; (g) Raman spectra of CLR and C*-T; (h) EPR spectra of CLR and C*-12; (i) C 1s and (g) O 1s XPS spectra of CLR and C*-T.

Electrochemical and catalytic activity tests of sacrificial anodes. (a) LSV curves; (b) Tafel slopes; (c) EIS curves (1.80 V vs. RHE); (d) electrolysis at constant potential of 1.80 V vs. RHE for 10 h; (e) H2 production rates; (f) Faradaic efficiencies; (g) color change of electrolyte in anode chamber before and after C*-12 electrolysis; (h) ultraviolet spectra of Ds12-n; (i) fluorescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm; (j) 13C NMR spectrum of Ds-12-4; (k) TEM and HRTEM images of Ds-12-4; (l) yield of CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Evolution of electrode structure and mechanism of SA reaction with H2O. (a) C 1s XPS spectra of C*-12 before and after electrolysis; (b) fa and ID1/IG values of C*-12 before and after electrolysis; (c) EPR spectrum of C*C-12-4; (d) SA structure; (e–g) adsorption energy and the number of transferred electrons of the SA model and H2O molecule; (h) reaction pathway of SA with H2O; (i) CDs yields achieved in hydroxyl quenching experiments.

head.ce:title: Self-sacrificing carbon electrode-controlled oxidation-enhanced water electrolysis for co-production of low-cost hydrogen and carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Inner Mongolia, Hohhot, 010051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Inner Mongolia, Hohhot, 010051, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-assisted water electrolysis can reduce the energy consumption of hydrogen production. In traditional carbon-assisted water electrolysis, carbon particles are directly introduced into the anode cell, but the speed at which these carbon particles are oxidized limits the hydrogen production efficiency of this process. Moreover, the excessive oxidation of carbon particles can produce CO2, leading to greater carbon emissions. Herein, a novel sacrificial anode (SA) based on the carbon particles in coal liquefaction residue is prepared. The carbon-oxygen reaction is controlled on the surface of SA to increase the hydrogen generation rate, and carbon quantum dots (CDs) are also produced by this reaction. A DFT investigation reveals that carbon atoms with a high spin density lower the adsorption energy of the reactive oxygen intermediate ·OH during the transition from OH− to ·OH. Carbon-rich clusters on the sacrificial anode are in-situ oxidized by ·OH to form CDs. Additionally, at a potential of 1.80 V, the energy consumption required for hydrogen production can be reduced to 2.15–2.58 kWh/Nm3, and the corresponding yield of CDs is as high as 13.66 wt%. This research provides a new horizon for large-scale, low-cost, and green hydrogen production via carbon-assisted water electrolysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is a clean high-energy-density renewable energy source that is expected to replace fossil energy, solving the energy crisis and environmental problems [1]. The utilization of water electrolysis for hydrogen production shows vast application potential [2,3]. The four-electron oxygen evolution reaction (OER: 2H2O→2H2 + O2, E0 = 1.23 V) at the anode accounts for 90% of the energy consumption in hydrogen production via water electrolysis, while the two-electron hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode consumes much less energy [4–6]. Carbon-assisted water electrolysis replaces this OER process with a carbon oxidation reaction (COR: C + 2H2O → CO2 + 4H2; E0 = 0.21 V), thereby achieving a reduction in energy consumption.

Traditionally, carbon-assisted water electrolysis processes add a carbon source to the electrolyte to form a coal water slurry system [7–10]. In these processes, the oxidation reaction is utilized to produce reactive oxygen species (e.g., ·OH), which pass through the anode surface and transfer to the carbon particles in the electrolyte. Consequently, OER is replaced with COR. The production and reaction pathway of carbon-assisted electrolytic water for hydrogen reactive oxygen species is "electrode surface-electrolyte-carbon particle". However, the reactive oxygen species have a short lifetime, and the reaction pathway is long. Therefore, the reaction rate is excessively slow. The diffusion rate of carbon particles in the electrolyte is also slow, and the carbon slurry is unstable. This results in a difficult reverse COR reaction at high current densities. One strategy for improving the mass transfer rate involves utilizing noble metal materials (Pt, Ir, Ru) as the electrode [11–13]. However, precious metals are expensive, and the generation of a passivation layer on the electrode surface leads to easy deactivation. Improving the oxidation degree of coal by regulating the electrolytic environment where the carbon particles are located can also lead to enhanced mass transfer. This can involve the application of ultrasonication, pressure, high temperatures, and other external field-strengthening mass transfer effects [14–16]. However, the COR conditions are complex and difficult to control, and changing different parameters leads to different strengthening effects.

Carbon-based sacrificial anode technology employs a self-supporting and self-sacrificing anode (SA) as the carbon source. Reactive oxygen species are produced at the solid-liquid two-phase interface of this sacrificial anode, and these species are directly used for the oxidation of carbon at the electrode. Therefore, mass transfer over long distances and long periods is avoided, which effectively increases the reaction rate. Moreover, the SA replaces the precious metal anode used for the electrolysis of water to produce hydrogen, significantly cutting costs. Notably, when the appropriate carbon source is selected as the raw material, the SA will exhibit a high level of carbon radical production. By controlling the reaction conditions, this anode can be used to generate high-value-added products through induced carbon-free radical polymerization.

Coal liquefaction residue (CLR) is characterized by its high carbon content and primarily consists of heavy oils, asphaltene, and pre-asphaltenes [17,18]. The main body of the CLR aromatic structure is similar to the core unit of the raw coal structure model, and heat treatment can provide CLR with conductivity and defect structures. Research has shown that CLR is a promising electrode material for the next generation of energy storage devices, such as batteries and supercapacitors [19,20]. CLR is highly viscous and easily moldable, enhancing electrode stability. Simultaneously, CLR is notable for its abundant microcrystalline carbon regions, making CLR a good raw material for the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CDs) via chemical oxidation [21–23]. Consequently, employing CLR as a fundamental component of SA is expected to enhance hydrogen evolution while also enabling the preparation of CDs.

In this study, coal liquefaction residue was used to construct a carbon electrode that combines HER with a self-supporting COR reaction to generate hydrogen and prepare carbon quantum dots. The feasibility of this electrode was electrochemically demonstrated. Various characterization methods and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to study the evolution of the carbon structure in the COR reaction and analyze the COR pathway. This study aims to provide insights into the high-value utilization of coal liquefaction residue via carbon-assisted electrolysis for hydrogen production.

CLR from Shenhua Group in Ordos City, Inner Mongolia, China (particle size of 0.2 mm) was selected as the raw material for the experiments. Analytical-grade potassium hydroxide (Tianjin Xinbot Chemicals), potassium bromide (Shanghai McLin Biochemical Technology), tert-butanol (Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), and polyvinylidene fluoride (Shanghai Aichun Biological Technology) were used as the reagents.

CLR (10 g) was pyrolyzed in a tube furnace (SYD-T116) with a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a 200 mL/min N2 flow. Different CLR samples were heated from room temperature to 600, 900, 1200, and 1400 °C for 2 h (Fig. 1 ). After cooling to room temperature, samples were denoted as C-T, where T values of 6, 9, 12, and 14 correspond to pyrolysis temperatures of 600, 900, 1200, and 1400 °C, respectively.

10% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was added to each C-T sample, and each mixture was ground for 5 min to ensure even mixing. Then, 1.1000 g (±0.0001 g) of each mixture was weighed and poured into a tableting mold (φ = 16 mm). A tableting machine was used to apply a pressure of 22 ± 1 MPa for 1 min to obtain a liquefied residue coke electrode, which was denoted as C*-T.

An electrochemical workstation (DH7000, Jiangsu) was used to electrochemically evaluate the C*-T electrodes. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed on C*-T over the potential range of 0–3 V vs. RHE with a scanning rate of 10 mV/s. Additionally, Tafel polarization curves (Tafel) were obtained for C*-T using a step height of 0.1 mV and a step time of 0.1 s. AC impedance analysis (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS) was conducted on C*-T over the frequency range of 0.1–104 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mV. The acquired AC impedance data were processed using ZView software to fit the data to an experimental model circuit. Furthermore, under a working potential of 1.80 V vs. RHE, continuous electrolysis was carried out for n × 3600 s to assess the electrochemical performance of C*-T. The gas produced at the anode and cathode was analyzed using gas chromatography. All potentials were converted to the potential of the reversible hydrogen electrode (VRHE = VHg/HgO + 0.0592 × pH + 0.098).

The optical properties of the samples were studied using a UV spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Japan) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A, USA). The morphological organization, size, and distribution characteristics of the samples were studied using a scanning electron microscope (SEM; Hitachi Regulus 8220, Japan) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM; Talos 200X, USA). The pore size distributions of the samples were analyzed using an automatic specific surface area and pore size analyzer (3H–2000PS1/2, China). The molecular structures, chemical bonds, and functional group variations were studied using a Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (IRTracer-100, Japan), laser micro-Raman spectrometer (INVIA REFLEX, UK), X-ray diffractometer (XRDynamic 500, USA), and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). Carbon-13 nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) analysis was conducted in a Bruker AVANCE III 400 MHz solid-state spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was conducted on a Bruker EMX PLUS instrument (Germany) to examine the persistent free radicals (PFRs) in the samples. Gas compositions were analyzed using an Agilent gas chromatograph (GC; 7890B, USA). Measurement details are available in the supplementary materials.

The theoretical calculations were conducted with the Dmol3 module in Materials Studio 2019. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was employed to deal with the exchange-correlation interactions. The ultrasoft pseudopotentials were used with a kinetic cut-off energy of 400 eV, which was applied to describe the expansion of the electronic eigenfunctions [24,25]. The vacuum layer thickness was set to be at least 15 Å to minimize interlayer interactions. The Brillouin-zone integration was sampled by a 6 × 6 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-point. The adsorption energies (Eads) were calculated using the equation Eads = Ead/sub − Ead − Esub, where Ead/sub, Ead, and Esub are the total energies of the optimized adsorbate/substrate system, the adsorbate in the structure, and the clean substrate, respectively.

The structural characteristics of the prepared sacrificial anodes were analyzed using various material characterization methods. The structure and morphology of the samples were investigated through SEM and TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 2a, the C*-12 sample, which was obtained by the pyrolysis of CLR at 1200 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere, exhibits an irregular block structure with a few pores. This configuration is advantageous for enhancing contact between the catalyst and the electrolyte. Additionally, the morphology and structure of the samples were further examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. S2 demonstrates that the CLR sample has an amorphous carbon structure devoid of distinct lattice fringes. Upon pyrolysis and carbonization at 1200 °C, an annual ring structure is generated in the C*-12 sample. This structure contains disordered carbon surrounded by an ordered carbon framework, which is primarily composed of ordered carbon (Fig. 2b–d). This observation suggests that heat treatment facilitates the generation of new defect structures in the CLR material during the graphitization process. The specific surface area and pore structure of the samples were further analyzed using nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis. The surface areas of the samples increase with increasing carbonization temperature, following the order: CLR (2.15 m2/g) < C*-6 (2.78 m2/g) < C*-9 (3.18 m2/g) < C*-12 (3.53 m2/g) < C*-14 (5.10 m2/g). Additionally, the mesoporous structure also increases with increasing temperature (Fig. 2e and f). The presence of a rich mesoporous structure facilitates the transmission of the reaction medium and should significantly enhance the catalytic effect of the COR reaction.

Raman spectroscopy offers a significant advantage in distinguishing between crystalline and amorphous carbon in carbon materials. As illustrated in Fig. 2g, all samples exhibit two prominent characteristic peaks at 1345 cm−1 (D band, indicative of defective graphite structures) and 1590 cm−1 (G band, representative of crystalline graphite). The degree of graphitization and the number of defect sites in each sample [26,27] were assessed by calculating the ID1/IG ratio. This analysis indicates that the ID1/IG ratio increases with increasing carbonization temperature. Notably, after pyrolysis and carbonization at 1400 °C, the C*-14 sample contains equivalent amorphous carbon and graphite carbon content. This observation further suggests that heat treatment enables the development of new defect structures when CLR is graphitized.

The pyrolysis process of coal or biomass can induce the cleavage of covalent bonds. This results in the formation of atoms or groups with unpaired electrons, which are referred to as persistent free radicals (PFRs) [28,29]. As illustrated in Fig. 2h, the EPR spectrum of C*-12 shows a distinct Lorentzian line characteristic peak signal between 3500 and 3520 G. This peak is associated with the unpaired electrons of sp2 carbon. The XPS C 1s spectra of the samples can be deconstructed into five peaks representing the CC bond (sp2) at 284.8 eV, C–C bond (sp3) at 285.5 eV, CO bond at 286.5 eV, and O–CO bond at 288.5 eV. As the carbonization temperature increases, stable functional groups within the carbon skeleton (such as C–O, CO, and O–CO) are removed, leading to the formation of new carbon structures. This observation is further corroborated by the O 1s spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and catalytic activity of SA
ce:section.ce:para: A three-electrode system was employed to evaluate the room-temperature COR activity of the sample under alkaline conditions. Within the same electrolyte, the initial potential of the LSV curves of the CLR*-T carbon electrodes range from 0.33 to 0.42 V vs. RHE (Fig. 3a). These values are significantly lower than the initial potential of the Pt electrode (1.80 V vs. RHE). This observation suggests that the C*-T electrodes exhibit superior electrochemical performance, with the exception of C*-6. Within the potential range of 1.20–1.80 V, the C*-14 electrode demonstrates a higher current density compared to C*-9 and C*-12 (Fig. S4). This can be attributed to the heat treatment process, which facilitates the formation of a new carbon structure with active sites during the graphitization process that improves the COR performance.

The Tafel slope is closely related to the rate of electron transfer [30,31]. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, the Tafel slopes of C*-9, C*-12, and C*-14 are 139 mV/dec, 95 mV/dec, and 85 mV/dec, respectively. This trend indicates that increasing the carbonization temperature enhances the kinetics of the electrochemical reactions. Furthermore, the kinetics of the COR reaction at the electrode-electrolyte interface were assessed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 3c, at an anode potential of 1.80 V vs. RHE, increasing the carbonization temperature leads to a decline in the semicircle radius, suggesting a reduction in charge transfer resistance (Rct) and an accelerated electrochemical reaction rate. Notably, C*-14 exhibits the lowest Rct value of 1.32 Ω (Fig. S4)

Fig. 3d illustrates the constant-potential electrolysis curves of the C*-T electrodes. After electrolysis for 10 h at an anode potential of 1.80 V vs. RHE, C*-9, C*-12, and C*-14 have current densities of approximately 3.62 mA/cm2, 21.56 mA/cm2, and 25.08 mA/cm2, respectively. When the electrolysis time is extended beyond 10 h, the current density of C*-12 begins to decrease, demonstrating that the carbon source is consumed with increasing electrolysis time. However, within the entire 50 h test, the current density of this electrode only decrease by about 10 mA/cm2. Therefore, this carbon electrode has good stability and long-term performance (Fig. S5). Compared with some conventional metal anode electrodes, the carbon-based anode electrodes have a short service life. The low cost and ease of processing in scenarios that require short-term hydrogen production (such as laboratory studies or temporary hydrogen production equipment) of the carbon-based anode may make it a suitable choice.

To demonstrate that the carbon-sacrificial anode technique can improve the mass transfer efficiency of the electrochemical process, the C*-12 carbon electrode prepared from coal liquefaction residue was compared with the same mass of coal water slurry (25 g/L) prepared from coal liquefaction residue at a constant voltage. As shown in Fig. S6, the current density of the C*-12 carbon electrode prepared using coal liquefaction residue is much greater than the current density of the coal water slurry system. This indicates that utilizing the coal liquefaction residue carbon electrode can improve the mass transfer efficiency. Fig. 3e presents the hydrogen production rates at each sacrificial anode. With a starting potential of 1.80 V vs. RHE, the Pt anode exhibits a hydrogen production rate of 0 mL h−1 cm−2. In contrast, the C*-9, C*-12, and C*-14 anodes demonstrate hydrogen production rates of 4.12 mL h−1 cm−2, 11.56 mL h−1 cm−2, and 13.17 mL h−1 cm−2, respectively. This indicates that the sacrificial anode design significantly enhances the hydrogen production rate of the cathode, with the C*-14 anode yielding the highest rate. The Faraday efficiency of C*-14 is 93.61%, which is 1.19 and 1.03 times greater than that of C*-9 and C*-12, respectively (Fig. 3f). The energy consumption values of C*-9 and C*-12 are 2.58 kWh/Nm3 and 2.23 kWh/Nm3, respectively. Meanwhile, C*-14 achieves the lowest energy consumption of 2.15 kWh/Nm3. The energy consumption required for hydrogen production in this experiment is compared with that required for other water electrolysis or carbon-assisted water electrolysis methods in Table S1. This comparison indicates that the C*-12 anode exhibits lower energy consumption and superior hydrogen production efficiency.

To further investigate the characteristics of the COR reaction, gas chromatography was employed to analyze the gas composition of the C*-12 sample following the potentiostatic test. As illustrated in Fig. S8, this carbon electrode produces O2, CO2, and CO gases, indicating a reversal of the carbon oxidation reaction and that COR partially replaces OER. The oxygen-hydrogen ratio of the C*-14 electrode is 0.27, which is 46% lower than the theoretical value for the Pt electrode (0.5) [32]. Notably, as illustrated in Fig. S6 and Fig. 3g, the electrolyte solutions of all carbon anodes (except for the low-activity C*-6) gradually transition from colorless to dark yellow during the constant-potential electrolytic hydrogen production process. Notably, the C*-12 electrolyte exhibits a darker hue. However, this phenomenon is not observed when graphite is employed as the anode.

The optical properties of these exfoliated carbon layers stripped from the C*-12 electrode into the electrolyte were investigated by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. For clarity, the carbon layers obtained after n h of the reaction with C*-12 are labeled as Ds-12-n (where n = 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5). As shown in Fig. 3h, these carbon layers have absorption peaks at 230 nm and 350 nm, which are respectively ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bond and the n-π* transition [33,34] of the unsaturated carbon. Notably, Ds-12-4 has the highest absorbance values of 0.329 at 350 nm and 4.477 at 230 nm. In addition, the emission wavelength of these carbon layers at 350 nm is 460 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the carbon layer first increases and then decreases with electrolysis time. The possible structural composition of Ds-12-4 was further evaluated by XPS and 13C NMR analysis. Fig. S11 shows the fitted C 1s spectrum of Ds-12-4. Based on this spectrum, the oxygen-containing groups of Ds-12-4 are C–O and O–CO. Meanwhile, the 13C NMR results indicate that this sample can be generally divided into four main chemical regions. The chemical shifts of aromatic protonated carbon, aromatic bridgehead carbon, alkyl-substituted carbon, and carbonyl carbon are 105 ppm, 126–130 ppm, 135–144 ppm, and 175–190 ppm, respectively (Fig. 3j). These results indicate that Ds-12-4 predominantly consists of aromatic nuclear and carboxyl functional groups.

The microstructures of the exfoliated carbon layers were also investigated using TEM. As shown in Fig. 3k, the exfoliated carbon layers have an average size of 2.89 nm and an average lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. The chemical and physical properties of these exfoliated carbon layers are similar to those of carbon quantum dots (CDs). CDs can be applied in the fields of biomedicine, optoelectronic materials, ion detection, and catalysis [35,36]. The yields of the CDs were calculated, and the highest CDs yield of 13.66 wt% was achieved after 40 h of electrolysis (Fig. 3l). Additionally, Table S2 presents a comparison of CDs synthesized using electrochemical methods with different materials. This comparison underscores the superiority of the CDs electrochemically synthesized in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evolution of electrode structure and clarification of COR mechanisms of sacrificial anode
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the electro-oxidation characteristics of the prepared carbon electrodes, the morphology and microstructure of the samples at 1.80 V vs. RHE were examined using SEM after the chronoamperometry test. As displayed in Fig. S12, C*-12 shows some laminar structures after electrolysis. This is because the free radicals generated by the mesoporous carbon during electrolysis can easily break the chemical bonds of the carbon, opening the original carbon ring structure. The fitted C 1s spectra obtained before and after the electrolysis of C*-12 are displayed in Fig. 4a. The proportion of oxidized compounds in the carbon structure gradually increases throughout the electrolysis process, rising from 13.12% to 71.52%. This indicates the continuous oxidation of carbon throughout the entire electrolysis process.

Some of the structures of coal liquefaction residue coke exhibit similarities to graphite, making X-ray diffraction (XRD) a suitable method for analyzing the carbon structure of C*-12 before and after electrolysis. The lattice structure characteristics were determined by fitting the XRD pattern, as illustrated in Fig. S13. For ease of description, the electrolytic electrode is denoted as C*C-12-n (n = 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5). C*C-12-n exhibits a broad C (002) peak at about 25.80° [37]. The parameters derived from the XRD fitting analysis were computed using specific formulas, as detailed in Table S3. Interestingly, the aromaticity (fa) displays a fluctuating pattern with electrolysis time, initially decreasing and then increasing. For instance, fa decreases from 0.8132 to 0.6497 after 20 h, then abruptly rises to 0.7637 after 30 h. As shown in Fig. 4b, the ID1/IG ratio demonstrates a pattern of initially increasing and then sharply decreasing, aligning with the XRD analysis. This indicates that the carbon oxidation process of this electrode involves alternating oxidation. Analysis of the EPR spectral data (Fig. 4c) reveals that the characteristic peak signal of PFRs in C*C-12-4 is more pronounced compared to that of the PFRs in C*-12. This enhancement can be attributed to the exfoliation of most oxygen-rich groups in the carbon skeleton during the continuous oxidation process, which leads to the production of PFRs. The experimentally observed abundance of PFRs in C*C-12-4 can be attributed to this process.

To elucidate the reaction mechanism of SA with H2O, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed. Existing literature has utilized an armchair carbon cluster composed of benzene rings as the structural model of high-temperature coke to study catalytic gasification reaction mechanisms [38]. Due to the large molecular weight of coke, it is reasonable to study a carbon cluster composed of about 14 benzene rings. The catalysis of the COR reaction is associated with unpaired radicals, as observed by characterization techniques. Considering the actual lattice fringe size observed in the HRTEM image of C*-12, a carbon cluster consisting of 13 benzene rings with an unpaired electron was adopted as a model for the DFT calculations (Fig. 4d). In this study, this model was only used to study the adsorption of unpaired carbon and the reaction process with H2O.

The affinity of C*-12 for H2O was investigated by calculating the adsorption energy and the number of transferred electrons of the SA model and the H2O molecules. As illustrated in Fig. 4e–g, the adsorption energy of H2O on C1 (−1.85 eV) is lower than that of H2O on C2 (−0.22 eV) and C3 (−0.14 eV). Additionally, the adsorption energies of H2O on C4 and C5 (located in the middle of the carbon cluster) and those of C6 and C7 (situated at the farthest distances from C1) are greater than −1.85 eV (Fig. S14). This suggests that the unpaired electron carbon atoms are strongly hydrophilic. Furthermore, band charge analysis was used to evaluate the actual number of transferred electrons. C1 can capture more electrons from H2O (0.303 e−) compared to the other carbon atoms.

Next, the reaction pathway involving the SA model with H2O molecules was analyzed to investigate the activity of C*-12 and the essential role of PFRs in the electrocatalytic oxidation of carbon. As illustrated in Fig. 4h, the thermodynamic feasibility of a hydroxyl ion (OH−) losing one electron to generate a hydroxy radical (·OH) is minimal when catalyzed by unpaired electron carbon atoms, with the formation of C–OH occurring at the C1 carbon atom. Moreover, the yield of CDs decreases when the hydroxy quencher tert-butanol (TBA) is introduced into the electrolyte, suggesting that C*-12 generates ·OH during the COR reaction (Fig. 4i). In reaction pathway COR1, C–OH continues to evolve into CO through the action of ·OH. Subsequently, the hydroxide can lose one electron to yield ·OH through the catalytic oxidation of CO, leading to the formation of C-*OH with the C2 carbon atom. Next, the open loop cleaves the C1–C2 bond to generate C–OH and OC–OH, ultimately resulting in the production of CO2. In route COR2, the complete process for generating CDs requires an energy input of 5.64 eV. This also indicates that when the unpaired carbon structure is involved in the COR reaction, CO2 is predominantly produced, while electricity is used to provide a continuous energy supply for the generation of CDs.

In summary, carbon-based sacrificial anodes were prepared by treating coal liquefaction residue with a simple carbonization process. The optimal carbon-based sacrificial anode demonstrates remarkable electrocatalytic carbon oxidation activity. Compared to previous methods utilizing biomass as a carbon source, the hydrogen production rate achieved in this work is approximately 13.17 mL h−1 cm−2, while the energy consumption for hydrogen production is reduced to 2.15–2.58 kWh/Nm3. Additionally, the prepared anode can generate a carbon quantum dot content of 13.66 wt%, significantly enhancing the added value of the sacrificial anode in promoting hydrogen evolution. Various characterization analyses and DFT calculations indicate that carbon oxidation occurs in a two-stage "oxidation-stripping" process. ·OH radicals is crucial for the completion of the overall carbon oxidation reaction. This work provides a new strategy for the low-cost generation of hydrogen and the production of carbon dots via carbon-based energy conversion.

Ruzhan Bai: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Na Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Nana Ji: Investigation, Formal analysis. Dexu Li: Investigation, Data curation. Quansheng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Huacong Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Yingyue Teng: Methodology. Jianxiu Hao: Formal analysis. Yanpeng Ban: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work. There is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21868021, 22468035 and 22308048), the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (2022MS02007), and the Odors Science and Technology Project (2021YY-GONG-119-49).

ce:section.ce:para:
